jokes.text: Bobby excitedly shows his play review to the gang
jokes.setup: Bobby is excited about his play review and wants everyone to see it
jokes.punchline: The review calls the play 'a dog' - the opening line is devastating
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection - Bobby's excitement contrasts with the brutal opening of the review. Economy is excellent with 'a dog' being perfectly dismissive.

jokes.text: Don't let this go to your head.
jokes.setup: Bobby is reading a devastating review
jokes.punchline: Someone sarcastically warns him not to get cocky about terrible reviews
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan sarcasm delivered with great economy. Works as a reality check moment.

jokes.text: Bobby finds the one positive mention of his performance in an otherwise scathing review
jokes.setup: After all that criticism, Bobby insists they keep reading
jokes.punchline: There's actually a glowing review of Bobby's performance buried in the carnage
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure - completely reverses our expectations and justifies Bobby's excitement. The contrast is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: The only thing that's bothering me is I always thought this critic was a jerk. I guess I was wrong.
jokes.setup: Bobby has just been praised by a critic
jokes.punchline: He admits he thought the critic was terrible until the critic praised him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing how praise changes our perspective. The honesty of the admission makes it funnier than if he'd been defensive.

jokes.text: Tony's increasingly desperate attempts to make Brian's getting hit by a pitch sound strategic
jokes.setup: Brian keeps deflating Tony's heroic narrative
jokes.punchline: Tony claims Brian 'psyched out' the pitcher by striking out four times
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's character perfectly - his need to build Brian up even when the facts don't support it. The 'made him cocky' logic is absurd but endearing.

jokes.text: Rich kids have rich parents.
jokes.setup: Someone asks why Brian hangs out with rich kids
jokes.punchline: Brian states the obvious with perfect kid logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character-specific humor. Brian's matter-of-fact delivery of obvious logic is both funny and revealing of his practical mindset.

jokes.text: They do to adopt me. With a face like this, I'm shooting for big bucks.
jokes.setup: Someone says you don't need to be rich to adopt kids
jokes.punchline: Brian matter-of-factly explains his looks require wealthy parents
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character writing - a 9-year-old's brutally honest assessment of his adoptability. The dark humor is perfectly balanced with Brian's pragmatic personality.

jokes.text: The dimples inspection sequence
jokes.setup: Brian claims his dimples are his selling point
jokes.punchline: Tony examines them in different lighting like evaluating merchandise
jokes.explanation: Decent visual gag that plays into Brian's commodification of his cuteness and Tony's willingness to go along with it.

jokes.text: Yeah, in this racket you're over the hill at seven.
jokes.setup: Discussion about how adoption gets harder as kids get older
jokes.punchline: Someone treats adoption like a career with a short prime
jokes.explanation: Dark but effective humor that treats adoption like show business. The 'racket' terminology and age cutoff is both funny and sadly realistic.

jokes.text: Brian's age deception being discovered
jokes.setup: Brian claims to be eight but someone mentions his social worker said he's nine
jokes.punchline: Brian's relief that his age 'doesn't show' like it's a physical trait
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Brian's awareness of his 'marketing' challenges. Treating age like a visible defect is absurd but fits his mindset.

jokes.text: I have parents.
jokes.setup: Alex asks if Tony has ever thought about adoption
jokes.punchline: Tony completely misunderstands, thinking Alex meant Tony being adopted
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that works because Tony's confusion feels natural. The economy is excellent - just three words completely derail the conversation.

jokes.text: Sure. I went to grade school with hundreds of them.
jokes.setup: Asked if he's spent much time around kids
jokes.punchline: Tony counts his own childhood as child-care experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony logic - he finds a way to make his completely irrelevant experience sound relevant. The 'hundreds of them' makes it sound like expertise.

jokes.text: Oh, that's 11 years' experience.
jokes.setup: Tony claims grade school as child experience
jokes.punchline: Someone sarcastically treats it as legitimate experience and calculates the years
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan response that plays along with Tony's absurd logic while highlighting its ridiculousness.

jokes.text: Bobby's second bad review revelation
jokes.setup: Bobby seemed to have one good review earlier
jokes.punchline: Now the Long Island Bulletin destroyed his performance too
jokes.explanation: Solid callback that deflates Bobby's earlier triumph. The pattern of reviewing his reviews becomes its own running gag.

jokes.text: Hey, Wheeler, I brought you some Rolaids.
jokes.setup: The review mentioned Bobby's 'indigestion' from scenery chewing
jokes.punchline: Louie brings antacids as a literal interpretation of the metaphor
jokes.explanation: Excellent Louie moment - takes a metaphorical criticism literally to twist the knife. Perfect character integration and economy.

jokes.text: And Jim becau... Did I invite you?
jokes.setup: Tony is explaining why he invited everyone to help with Brian
jokes.punchline: Tony realizes mid-sentence he didn't actually invite Jim
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's social awkwardness and Jim's habit of inserting himself into situations.

jokes.text: No. I thought it was an oversight.
jokes.setup: Tony realizes he didn't invite Jim
jokes.punchline: Jim calmly explains he assumed it was a mistake
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim character moment - his matter-of-fact response to being uninvited shows his obliviousness and confidence.

jokes.text: Wow, did you burn that all by yourself?
jokes.setup: Tony has clearly burned lunch badly
jokes.punchline: Someone treats the burning as an accomplishment worth noting
jokes.explanation: Decent observational humor about Tony's cooking disaster. The 'all by yourself' implies it took real effort to burn it that badly.

jokes.text: Yeah, from scratch.
jokes.setup: Asked if he burned the food himself
jokes.punchline: Tony takes pride in burning it 'from scratch' like it's a cooking achievement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony response - he's so eager to be a good father that he takes pride even in spectacular failures. The 'from scratch' makes it sound artisanal.

jokes.text: Oh, sure, that's easy for you to say. All you had to do to get kids was give birth.
jokes.setup: Elaine is reassuring Tony about adoption being easy
jokes.punchline: Tony acts like natural childbirth is the 'easy way' compared to adoption
jokes.explanation: Brilliant perspective flip - Tony genuinely sees pregnancy and childbirth as easier than adoption paperwork. Perfect character moment showing his anxiety.

jokes.text: Gee, I feel so guilty I took the simple way out.
jokes.setup: Tony called giving birth 'easy'
jokes.punchline: Elaine sarcastically apologizes for taking the 'simple' route of pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Perfect sarcastic comeback that highlights the absurdity of Tony's statement. Elaine's dry delivery would be ideal here.

jokes.text: Tony, the only other room is the bathroom.
jokes.setup: Tony wants everyone to wait in another room for privacy
jokes.punchline: His apartment only has a bathroom as the other room
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor about Tony's small apartment. Sets up the absurd situation of hiding in the bathroom.

jokes.text: Okay, but I better warn you... I'm good at this.
jokes.setup: Everyone is going to hide in the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Jim thinks they're playing hide and seek and warns he's skilled at it
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jim moment - his childlike misunderstanding of the situation is perfectly in character. His confidence about being good at hide and seek is endearing.

jokes.text: When did you become a vegetarian? When I looked at this meat loaf.
jokes.setup: Brian claims to be vegetarian when offered Tony's meat loaf
jokes.punchline: He became vegetarian upon seeing Tony's burned disaster
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the cooking disaster with perfect timing. The immediacy of becoming vegetarian upon sight is great exaggeration.

jokes.text: You mean Princess Grace answered my letter?
jokes.setup: Tony suggests someone even better than the Brennans might want to adopt Brian
jokes.punchline: Brian thinks he meant Princess Grace has responded to his adoption letter
jokes.explanation: Perfect Brian moment - reveals he's written to royalty for adoption while treating it as a completely normal business inquiry. The specificity of Princess Grace is great.

jokes.text: They had to freshen up. Oh, yeah. You told me they do everything together.
jokes.setup: Tony explains why four people are in his bathroom
jokes.punchline: Brian accepts the absurd explanation and recalls Tony said they do everything together
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment where Brian's acceptance of the weird explanation feels natural for a kid who's seen everything.

jokes.text: Ready or not, here I come!
jokes.setup: Jim has been alone in the bathroom thinking they're playing hide and seek
jokes.punchline: He emerges ready to seek after the standard hide and seek countdown
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to Jim's hide and seek confusion. His commitment to the imaginary game after everyone left is pure Jim.

jokes.text: I've seen you outwitted by a pop-top can.
jokes.setup: Tony says Brian always outwits him in arguments
jokes.punchline: Alex points out Tony gets outwitted by inanimate objects
jokes.explanation: Brilliant insult that's both specific and universal. The image of Tony struggling with a pop-top can perfectly captures his character.

jokes.text: Alex repeats Tony's exact excuses back to him
jokes.setup: Tony lists reasons he can't go to the Brennans
jokes.punchline: When Tony asks Alex to go, Alex uses the identical excuses
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure where Alex mirrors Tony's excuses perfectly, exposing them as weak justifications. The 'number four' callback is perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, they got one in their house.
jokes.setup: The maid mentions Mr. Brennan is in the library
jokes.punchline: Tony is amazed they have a library in their house
jokes.explanation: Good class-conscious humor showing Tony's amazement at wealth. His wonder at a home library feels authentic to the character.

jokes.text: Who'd build a nice house like this next to a bunch of stables?
jokes.setup: Tony sees the wealthy estate
jokes.punchline: He assumes the house is the anomaly and the stables were there first
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony logic - he can't conceive of people wealthy enough to own horses, so he assumes the house is an intrusion on stable land.

jokes.text: Maybe the house was here first, and the horses moved in later.
jokes.setup: Tony wonders about the house/stable arrangement
jokes.punchline: Alex suggests the horses made a real estate decision
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist response that treats horses as autonomous real estate agents. Plays well with Tony's confusion.

jokes.text: I stretched it a little.
jokes.setup: Mr. Brennan mentions Brian said Tony has an impressive knockout record
jokes.punchline: Brian admits he exaggerated Tony's boxing credentials
jokes.explanation: Good understatement showing Brian's casual relationship with truth when it serves his purposes. Sets up Tony's embarrassing clarification.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. Once, I was out for almost an hour.
jokes.setup: Tony is asked about his impressive knockout record
jokes.punchline: His 'record' is being knocked unconscious, not knocking others out
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and character moment. Tony's pride in being unconscious shows his positive attitude despite his complete lack of boxing success.

jokes.text: And what do you do, Alex? Me? I drive a cab also. And you box, too? No, I just drive a cab.
jokes.setup: Mr. Brennan asks about Alex's occupation
jokes.punchline: Alex has no interesting hobbies or aspirations - just drives a cab
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - while Tony at least has boxing dreams, Alex is just entirely ordinary, making the conversation awkward.

jokes.text: Oh, Well, what do you want to do? Quit.
jokes.setup: Mr. Brennan asks about Alex's career aspirations
jokes.punchline: Alex's only aspiration is to quit his current job
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alex moment - his complete honesty about having no dreams beyond escaping his current situation. The one-word response is brilliant economy.

jokes.text: Last time I saw him, he was in Alaska selling batteries.
jokes.setup: Mr. Brennan asks what Muhammad Ali is doing these days
jokes.punchline: Tony thinks he's selling batteries in Alaska
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist response showing Tony's complete disconnect from celebrity culture. The specificity of 'Alaska selling batteries' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, that's the amount of brandy you're supposed to get.
jokes.setup: Tony offers to drink beer instead if they're short on brandy
jokes.punchline: He doesn't understand that expensive brandy is served in small amounts
jokes.explanation: Good class humor - Tony's unfamiliarity with how expensive alcohol is served reveals the cultural gap.

jokes.text: Well, it's a lot like boxing, only there are a lot fewer people around when you get beat up.
jokes.setup: Mr. Brennan asks what driving a cab is like
jokes.punchline: Tony compares it to boxing but notes the privacy of getting beaten up
jokes.explanation: Excellent Tony line combining his boxing obsession with his cabbie reality. The implication that he regularly gets beaten up in both is perfectly dark.

jokes.text: Before I came along, this was their idea of cute.
jokes.setup: Tommy, the Brennans' nephew, is introduced
jokes.punchline: Brian dismissively explains Tommy was the cute kid before his arrival
jokes.explanation: Perfect Brian confidence - he's completely displaced Tommy in the family's affections and he knows it. The dismissive assessment is brilliant.

jokes.text: Name three. Friends, happiness and health. Three that money can't buy. You're right. He's great.
jokes.setup: Alex challenges Brian to name things more important than money
jokes.punchline: Brian immediately names three that money can indeed buy, defeating Alex's argument
jokes.explanation: Brilliant comedy writing - Brian completely destroys Alex's moral argument by pointing out that money does buy friends, happiness, and health. Tony's defeated acknowledgment is perfect.

jokes.text: On your knees.
jokes.setup: Tony is struggling to make his adoption pitch to Brian
jokes.punchline: Alex literally tells Tony to get on his knees to beg
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy moment. Alex's directive creates a visual of Tony literally begging, which fits the emotional stakes.

jokes.text: Geez, what kind of choice is this for an eight-year-old? A nine-year-old. Oh, we're going to get nasty.
jokes.setup: Tony calls Brian eight during his heartfelt adoption speech
jokes.punchline: Brian corrects him to nine, and Tony threatens to 'get nasty' about the age lying
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the age lying with perfect timing. Tony's threat to 'get nasty' during his heartfelt speech shows his frustration.

jokes.text: Listen, if you ever think about adopting an older kid... Like, 42 years old... call me.
jokes.setup: Tony is leaving the Brennans after being rejected by Brian
jokes.punchline: He offers himself for adoption as a 42-year-old
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's hurt through humor. His offer to be adopted himself shows his genuine desire for a family.

jokes.text: Man, the traffic in this city is incredible!
jokes.setup: Brian appears at Tony's door unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: He casually complains about traffic like he just popped over for a visit
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Brian's casual entrance and complaint about traffic deflects from the dramatic moment of his return.

jokes.text: There's more important things in this world than money.
jokes.setup: Tony is amazed Brian came back
jokes.punchline: Brian repeats the exact moral lesson Alex tried to teach him earlier
jokes.explanation: Nice callback showing Brian has learned the lesson, though we soon discover his real motivation was different.

jokes.text: Oh, you're never going to believe this, but they didn't want to adopt me.
jokes.setup: Tony assumes Brian chose love over money
jokes.punchline: Brian reveals the wealthy family rejected him, so he had no choice
jokes.explanation: Excellent reversal that recontextualizes Brian's return. His casual delivery of devastating news is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I guess they got no taste.
jokes.setup: Tony asks why the Brennans didn't want Brian
jokes.punchline: Brian maintains his self-confidence despite being rejected
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Brian's unshakeable self-confidence means even rejection becomes their failure, not his.

jokes.text: So you're ready to settle for me now?
jokes.setup: Brian acts like he's doing Tony a favor by staying
jokes.punchline: Tony calls out Brian's attitude, pointing out he's now Tony's consolation prize
jokes.explanation: Good Tony moment where he maintains some dignity by calling out the obvious dynamic that he's Brian's backup plan.

jokes.text: Why don't you burn something up for dinner?
jokes.setup: Brian is making himself at home
jokes.punchline: He references Tony's terrible cooking from earlier
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to the cooking disaster. Shows Brian is comfortable enough to tease Tony about his failures.

jokes.text: Tony's rejection: 'I ain't going to be nobody's second choice'
jokes.setup: Tony realizing he's the backup plan after Brennan rejection
jokes.punchline: Tony's pride kicks in and he rejects Brian
jokes.explanation: Strong character moment showing Tony's pride and hurt feelings in realistic, relatable way.

jokes.text: Don't hit me with that smile, Brian. Dimples either.
jokes.setup: Brian is trying to charm his way back into Tony's good graces
jokes.punchline: Tony is now immune to the very features he found irresistible before
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback showing Tony's character growth - he's learned not to be manipulated by Brian's practiced charm offensive.

jokes.text: I love you, Tony.
jokes.setup: Tony is rejecting Brian and telling him to leave
jokes.punchline: Brian plays his ultimate emotional card
jokes.explanation: Perfect Brian manipulation - he saves the ultimate emotional weapon for last. The economy is perfect - three words that change everything.

jokes.text: There's such a thing as being too easy.
jokes.setup: Brian just said 'I love you' to avoid being kicked out
jokes.punchline: Tony recognizes he's being manipulated but can't resist
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony moment - he knows he's being played but can't help falling for it. Shows both his weakness and his self-awareness.

jokes.text: The card game resolution
jokes.setup: Tony suggests they settle the adoption question with cards
jokes.punchline: This supposedly eliminates emotion and manipulation from the decision
jokes.explanation: Good character solution - Tony tries to remove emotion and manipulation by making it random chance, which ironically shows how much he wants Brian to stay.
